sentences.text: That was my older sister, Shifra.
Right.
Mina.
And she was killed when the Germans came into our town.
That's Klevan in '41.
Are you talking about Klevan?
Yes.
Yeah.
We came to corpses were all over the place there.
And our people from the ghetto had to go and bury them in a massive grave.
And the-- where the synagogue, one synagogue was-- had a lot of people because people thought that it's a big building-- that they could hide there.
And they were bombed, you know, got killed there.
Yes, my younger sister.
Yes.
Yankel.
He was a child, young boy, maybe 2 and 1/2 years old at that time, 3.
I brought him up because my aunt was my father's sister.
They had business involved in, and I used to get paid to take care of him.
So after school, I went every day and used to take care of him.
And I brought him up.
It felt almost like my own child.
And before I went away to Rowne with my husband, I said goodbye to him.
And a child, he didn't understand.
He asked me when I'm coming back.
And I never saw him again.
I met people after the war that gave me pictures and told me how they were killed.
The kids were thrown in the graves half alive and buried that way.
And Yankel was one of them.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] Yeah.
And as I was telling you, when we came back to-- to Nowostaw, we were on the field, on the straw and hay hiding during the day.
And that man went to that woman that's now my sister-in-law and told her that we-- and that-- and she gave him food.
And her brothers asked her who he is.
And she said that he's a beggar, that he's came and he asked for some food.
And she thought-- felt sorry for him.
So she gave him food.
And told him that he should bring us when it get dark.
We had to cross a railroad.
And there was guards, German guards by the railroad.
And we had to make sure that, at that time, they went the other way, that we could pass.
And it's hard to explain or to really think how it happened.
But the only way that I can say is that it meant for us to survive.
And those things happened somehow, that everything went fast because I know a lot of people that tried to do what we did and didn't make it.
We came in to where that-- my sister-in-law's, not at that time, house.
She gave three knocks with her foot on the floor.
And Eli's brother came up from under the floor.
That was the brother that that was his girlfriend.
Yeah, Leva.
And we-- Eli-- and my husband paid the man.
And he went away.
We were in-- they took us down.
They had, under the floor, a big hole.
They dug out under the floor, and we were eight people there.
At that time, we were six.
It was Eli's younger brother and his wife.
David and Sonia.
And I-- and Leva was there, and a cousin, Abraham was there.
And I was with Eli there.
I came down, and we said that that's where we-- we tried to dig it deeper and further.
And we covered ourselves with the wall, with the dirt from-- that we dug out.
We had little shovels.
Yes.
Yes.
Right.
Very small.
Very small.
No.
You couldn't stand up.
It wasn't high enough to stand up.
But you can sit.
And no facilities.
Once a day, we had a pail that she used to take out from us.
Once a day, she used to give us a loaf of bread and a jug of soup.
And we used to share it.
For 18 months.
No.
Yeah.
No.
I said we were six at that time.
A couple months later, that we-- she used to have contact with the world.
She was outside.
And she used to bring us down a newspaper once in awhile.
No, there was no light.
But during the day, you can see a little bit.
The floor.
Floor.
No.
No.
There was incidents when her brothers went for some kind of reason under where we were, under the floor.
But being that we don't go out, and we covered ourselves in one area with the ground, that they never realized.
And as I said, two months later, two more people from our town knocked on her door at the middle of the night.
And when she saw them, she knew them.
She says, what are you doing here?
What are-- she says, [?
Bella, ?] somehow we feel that you know about the Lippers.
And you tell us where they are.
Maybe we can go with them.
Maybe we can hide with them.
And she was that kind of a person that she couldn't say no.
And she told them to stay and called us and led them down to us.
So that made us eight.
We stayed underground for 18 months.
Yeah.
Yeah.
You had to be careful.
You heard when somebody was around.
So we knew that when somebody was around.
The only person that used to go up at night was the brother that his girlfriend was there.
He used to go up at night.
No.
Just a dug out hole, a big one.
I would say we put three on one side.
No, we were eight, four on one side, and three on the other side.
We couldn't stretch our legs without touching the other people.
We always stayed in our clothes.
There was no washing or anything or showers, so anything like that.
For me was the worst part, I came in a young child, my blossom of my years.
I was maybe 15 at that time or 16.
And I, from under nourished and no light, I lost my hair.
I-- I didn't eat for months.
I couldn't eat.
A little piece of bread that I had, I used to save it, and I couldn't.
Here, eat it.
Very little.
Very little.
For anything, my sister-in-law from the brother that was there used to talk that recipes, when she'll go out, when she'll be free, what she's gonna cook.
She was older than I, much older.
She-- how she's gonna cook and what she's gonna make and things like that.
I really-- all kind of crazy things, chicken soup and [INAUDIBLE] and all kind of things.
Her father was a butcher.
And she really knew about life more than I did.
I knew very little.
[SIGHS] I really don't remember.
I can't comprehend now that we can so-- you-- a human being can survive for 18 months in those circumstances.
But it happened.
It happened.
I-- nothing, nothing at night.
Same thing.
There was day and night, except at night was pitch dark.
You heard mice running through.
Yes.
Yes.
We kept-- we were informed.
We had newspapers.
And I remember that they were talking that when America opened the second front, Europe, the war, yes, we heard about it.
We knew what was going on.
But whether-- when we started to worry about is when the-- the Russians were chasing the Germans back.
And the railroad tracks, we could hear action on it.
They were withdrawing.
And we heard one day that they coming from the side, not with the regular main road, but on the side road.
The Russian army is coming and trying to cut off the Germans.
And one day, my sister-in-law came and told us that she saw Russian soldiers coming on horses and trying to hide in the woods there and so on.
And we were worrying that they going to bomb the railroads.
And we are right there.
My brother, at that time, not my sister-in-law yet, went to look for the-- for the Russian soldiers.
She tried.
People thought that she's going for different reasons, that she's going to look for a Russian soldier to get involved with or something.
But she went to look for the Russian soldiers.
But there was still the Germans at the railroad station-- on the railroad watching.
She went and told them that she's poor and she would-- if they can help her with some bread and sugar.
And the Russian soldiers, and so on.
And they used to chase her away because they weren't really occupying the territory at that time.
Until one day, she came and there were a couple people there that look like captains or higher ranked people.
And she says to her, she didn't have any choice.
She had to do something, so she asked them if they Jewish.
And so they ask her, are you Jewish?
She said no.
So one man got up and went over to her.
And he told her, I'm Jewish.
He was a Russian soldier?
He was a Russian, higher than a soldier.
And she said, I want to talk to you.
But I don't want to talk up front of the people.
I have to talk to you.
And she told him that if he would like, he should come to the house.
And they told us-- they told her that she-- they can't get involved with business.
I mean, he knew already.
She told him that she has Jews hiding and she needs help.
And he told her that it's not time yet.
She-- he can't do anything for her, to stay still, wait.
When the time will come, I'll help you out.
But now, it's just-- he says to her, we're not stable yet.
That was in the end of '43.
No, it was already the end of '43, the beginning of '44, maybe January of '44 or-- or February, I thought.
And a couple days later, we-- you saw activity on the railroad.
And she was telling us that the Russian are coming from the other side.
And they pushing, and Germany's retreating.
They saw the German soldiers are retreating.
Until one day, she came and told us that there aren't any more Russia-- German soldiers at the railroad track.
Until one night, there was a lot of fighting, a lot of shooting, a lot of activity going on.
And the following day, there were Russian soldiers all over that village.
And-- but the Germans still were there and fighting with the shooting was going on.
And that man, that Russian soldiers, she went to see him again.
And he told her that if we gonna make it over-- during this night, he says, then we'll be OK, that they expect help, that there is a lot of tanks coming and so on.
And if they'll make it over the night, they'll be OK.
Then she-- you will be able to talk about it to her.
And we heard a lot of activities that night.
And the following day, she went to see him.
And he made that day [? to which ?] her day is gonna come to see us.
Not to see us.
She didn't want to tell him where or what because she didn't trust him either.
But she told him that she wants, he should come to her house and she'll talk to him.
And he did came with another soldier.
And it-- they came with their ammunition, with their revolvers out because they thought maybe she's something setting them up.
They didn't trust her either.
When he came in and she knocked, we had that sign that if you want us to come up, you knock three times on the floor.
So they knocked on the floor.
And my husband's two older brothers went up and he met them.
With their-- with the-- I didn't see it, but they were talking about it with their guns aimed.
They didn't know who's coming up, but it was really brave from the Russian soldiers.
He was really at Jew in his heart.
And he knew what was going on with the Germans and the Jews.
So he wanted to help.
He took us out.
We all came out, and he talked to us.
He cried like a baby when he saw us coming out.
The six of them were all right.
They could get up and walk.
Myself and the cousin, Abraham Kershern, we couldn't walk.
At that time, they wanted us to get out of there.
They didn't know what's gonna happen, if the Germans push the Russians back.
And so they wanted us to go to Rowne, to that place.
That was the big city.
And you had no way of going by car or by bus or by railroad.
You had to walk.
And myself and that cousin, we walked out.
We couldn't hardly walk, and we were left in our town with non-Jewish people.
And we told them.
We didn't tell them where we were or what we were.
But we here, and that's it.
And everybody said that they knew that the Lippers have to be somewhere, that the Lippers weren't killed.
They kept count of the people were killed.
They think that they knew.
And they said that they knew that the Lippers have to be around.
The four of them, six of them, I should say, my-- and at that time, that's the-- my sister-in-law went with them too.
Bella went with them to Rowne.
Myself and my cousin, my husband's cousin, we were left in Klevan, in that town because we couldn't go.
And they went.
We went to a non-Jewish family that agreed to take us in.
Yes.
Yes, we-- we-- we were getting stronger.
And we were be able, but we stayed there, and they treated us very well.
And those are people that remembered us from before the war that--
No, I don't remember their names.
And we stayed there for a couple weeks until the situation stabilized.
And the Russian were-- they already for all over the-- you know, they knew the little towns.
They took over and everything.
And there wasn't any more Germans.
At that time, to Germans withdrew to Poland to that pact that they made with Stalin before the war to divide Poland.
They went to that border.
That was by the [? book, ?] the--
It's-- it was quite a bit from us.
It's half of the Poland.
The country was divided in two.
And the part that was on the Russian, we were there.
And the Germans went all the way there.
But the Russians didn't leave them there.
They were fighting them further afterwards.
But we were already free.
And that was in February, March of '44.
And my husband came back to the small town, to Klevan, where I was left.
And we lived there.
We started working.
I went to live in a house that was a friend of ours.
But it was a house that was left, survived the war.
So I was given one room in the back.
And the Ukraine family that lived in this house lived in the front.
And we started to go to work.
My husband worked in a post office.
And--
I worked in a office, in a Russian office [? by ?] the town, you know, nothing special because at that time, I went to Russian school.
I knew well-- Russian well.
And so did my husband.
So my husband drove a horse and wagon to the railroad every day to pick up the mail.
And I'd wait in their office.
And we stayed there.
And-- and I became pregnant.
And my daughter was born in '45, March '45.
Is Ann Lipper.
Now it's Dean.
OK.
